title: When combinations of humans and AI are useful: A systematic review and meta-analysis
study_design_description: A preregistered systematic review and meta-analysis of 106 experimental studies reporting 370 effect sizes, conducted in accord with Kitchenham guidelines and PRISMA standards. Studies were searched from the ACM Digital Library, Web of Science, and AIS eLibrary for publications between January 2020 and June 2023. Each study was required to include an original human-participants experiment evaluating the performance of humans alone, AI alone, and human–AI combinations. A three-level meta-analytic random-effects model was used to account for dependencies in effect sizes nested within experiments.
primary_task_description: The meta-analysis synthesized 370 unique effect sizes from 106 experiments examining task performance across three conditions: human alone, AI alone, and human–AI combinations. Tasks varied widely across studies and were coded by type (decision vs. creation), data type, output, and other characteristics. Performance was measured using quantitative metrics such as task accuracy, error, and quality.
population_description: The studies in the dataset come from different samples of people—some involving doctors, others crowdworkers, and still others students. Specific participant demographics varied across the 106 included experiments.

arms.description: Humans performing the task without AI assistance
AI systems performing the task without human involvement
Humans and AI working together to perform the task

outcomes.outcome_description: The human–AI group performs better than both the human alone and the AI alone, analogous to strong synergy in human groups. Measured as the standardized mean difference (Hedges' g) between the performance of the human–AI system and the baseline (whichever of human alone or AI alone performed better on average).
The human–AI group performs better than the human alone. Measured as the standardized mean difference (Hedges' g) between the performance of the human–AI system and the baseline of the human alone.
Accuracy of task performance, one of the quantitative measures used across studies

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone)
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: The overall pooled effect was negative (g = −0.23; t92 = −2.89; two-tailed P = 0.005; 95% confidence interval (CI), −0.39 to −0.07) and considered small according to conventional interpretations

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs human_alone
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: The human–AI systems performed significantly better than humans alone, and this pooled effect size was positive (g = 0.64; t98 = 11.87; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, 0.53 to 0.74) and medium to large

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), moderated by task type: decision tasks
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Among decision tasks—those in which participants decided between a finite set of options—the pooled effect size for human–AI synergy was significantly negative (g = −0.27; t104 = −3.20; two-tailed P = 0.002; 95% CI, −0.44 to −0.10), which indicates performance losses from combining humans and AI

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), moderated by task type: creation tasks
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Among creation tasks—those in which participants created some sort of open-response content—the pooled effect size for human–AI synergy was positive (g  = 0.19; t104 = 1.35; two-tailed P = 0.180; 95% CI, −0.09 to 0.48), pointing to synergy between humans and AI

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), moderated by relative performance: human alone outperformed ai alone
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: when the human alone outperformed the AI alone, the combined human–AI system outperformed both alone with an average pooled effect size for human–AI synergy of g = 0.46 (t104 = 5.06; two-tailed P  = 0.000; 95% CI, 0.28 to 0.66), a medium-sized effect

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), moderated by relative performance: ai alone outperformed human alone
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: when the AI alone outperformed the human alone, performance losses occurred in the combined system relative to the AI alone, with a negative effect size for human–AI synergy of g = −0.54 (t104 = −6.20; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, −0.71 to −0.37), a medium-sized effect

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs human_alone, moderated by relative performance: ai alone outperformed human alone
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: When the AI outperformed the human alone, greater performance gains tended to occur in the human–AI systems relative to the human alone, and the pooled effect size for human augmentation was positive and medium to large in magnitude (g = 0.74; t104 = 13.50; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, 0.63 to 0.85)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), sensitivity analysis clustering at paper level
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Using this approach, we found a comparable overall effect size for human–AI synergy (g  = −0.22; t67 = −2.46; two-tailed P = 0.017; 95% CI, −0.41 to −0.04) and for human augmentation (g = 0.65; t69 = 9.96; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, 0.52 to 0.78)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs human_alone, sensitivity analysis clustering at paper level
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Using this approach, we found a comparable overall effect size for human–AI synergy (g  = −0.22; t67 = −2.46; two-tailed P = 0.017; 95% CI, −0.41 to −0.04) and for human augmentation (g = 0.65; t69 = 9.96; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, 0.52 to 0.78)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), sensitivity analysis excluding outliers
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: We performed a sensitivity re-analysis on a dataset excluding these effect sizes, which resulted in similar effect sizes for human–AI synergy (g  = −0.25; t104 = −3.45; two-tailed P = 0.001; 95% CI, −0.39 to −0.11) and human augmentation (g = 0.60; t104 = 12.60; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, 0.50 to 0.69)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs human_alone, sensitivity analysis excluding outliers
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: We performed a sensitivity re-analysis on a dataset excluding these effect sizes, which resulted in similar effect sizes for human–AI synergy (g  = −0.25; t104 = −3.45; two-tailed P = 0.001; 95% CI, −0.39 to −0.11) and human augmentation (g = 0.60; t104 = 12.60; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, 0.50 to 0.69)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), sensitivity analysis excluding estimated effect sizes
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: we conducted a sensitivity re-analysis on a dataset that omits the effect sizes we estimated, using either WebPlotDigitizer or the information provided by the authors in their paper, and again we found almost identical results for human–AI synergy ( g = −0.21; t98 = −2.59; two-tailed P  = 0.011; 95% CI, −0.36 to −0.05) and human augmentation (g = 0.64; t98 = 11.73; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, 0.53 to 0.75)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs human_alone, sensitivity analysis excluding estimated effect sizes
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: we conducted a sensitivity re-analysis on a dataset that omits the effect sizes we estimated, using either WebPlotDigitizer or the information provided by the authors in their paper, and again we found almost identical results for human–AI synergy ( g = −0.21; t98 = −2.59; two-tailed P  = 0.011; 95% CI, −0.36 to −0.05) and human augmentation (g = 0.64; t98 = 11.73; two-tailed P = 0.000; 95% CI, 0.53 to 0.75)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: ai_alone vs human_alone vs human_ai_combination, fake hotel review detection task
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: For fake hotel review detection, the researchers found that the AI alone achieved an accuracy of 73%, the human alone achieved an accuracy of 55% and the human–AI system achieved an accuracy of 69%

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: ai_alone vs human_alone vs human_ai_combination, bird image classification task
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: for bird image classification, the AI alone achieved an accuracy of 73%, the human alone achieved an accuracy of 81% and the human–AI system achieved an accuracy of 90%

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), moderation test by task type
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: we found that the type of task significantly moderated human–AI synergy (F 1,104 = 7.84, two-tailed P = 0.006)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), moderation test by data type
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: the type of data involved in the task significantly moderated both human–AI synergy (F 4,101 = 15.24, two-tailed P = 0.000) and human augmentation (F4,101 = 6.52, two-tailed P = 0.000)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs human_alone, moderation test by data type
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: the type of data involved in the task significantly moderated both human–AI synergy (F 4,101 = 15.24, two-tailed P = 0.000) and human augmentation (F4,101 = 6.52, two-tailed P = 0.000)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), moderation test by relative performance of human vs AI
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: we found that the performance of the human and AI relative to each other impacted both human–AI synergy (F1,104 = 81.79, two-tailed P = 0.000) and human augmentation ( F1,104  = 24.35, two-tailed P = 0.000)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs human_alone, moderation test by relative performance of human vs AI
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: we found that the performance of the human and AI relative to each other impacted both human–AI synergy (F1,104 = 81.79, two-tailed P = 0.000) and human augmentation ( F1,104  = 24.35, two-tailed P = 0.000)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), moderation test by type of AI
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: we found that the type of AI involved in the experiment (F2,103  = 3.77, two-tailed P  = 0.026) and the year of publication (F3,102 = 3.65, two-tailed P = 0.015) moderated human–AI synergy, and the experimental design moderated human augmentation (F1,104 = 4.90, two-tailed P = 0.029)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), moderation test by year of publication
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: we found that the type of AI involved in the experiment (F2,103  = 3.77, two-tailed P  = 0.026) and the year of publication (F3,102 = 3.65, two-tailed P = 0.015) moderated human–AI synergy, and the experimental design moderated human augmentation (F1,104 = 4.90, two-tailed P = 0.029)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs human_alone, moderation test by experimental design
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: we found that the type of AI involved in the experiment (F2,103  = 3.77, two-tailed P  = 0.026) and the year of publication (F3,102 = 3.65, two-tailed P = 0.015) moderated human–AI synergy, and the experimental design moderated human augmentation (F1,104 = 4.90, two-tailed P = 0.029)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs best of (human_alone or ai_alone), publication bias test Egger's regression
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Egger's regression did not indicate evidence of publication bias in the sample (β = −0.67; t104 = −0.78; two-tailed P = 0.438; 95% CI, −2.39 to 1.04), nor did the rank correlation test (τ = 0.05, two-tailed P = 0.121)

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: human_ai_combination vs human_alone, publication bias test Egger's regression
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: In this case, Egger's regression does point to publication bias in the sample (β = 1.96; t104 = 3.24; two-tailed P = 0.002; 95% CI, 0.76 to 3.16), as does the rank correlation test (τ = 0.19, two-tailed P = 0.000)

notes: This is a preregistered systematic review and meta-analysis, not a single original experiment. It synthesizes 370 effect sizes from 106 experimental studies published between January 2020 and June 2023. The primary finding is that human-AI combinations performed significantly worse than the best of humans or AI alone on average (g = −0.23, p = 0.005), but significantly better than humans alone (g = 0.64, p = 0.000). Key moderators include task type (decision tasks showed losses, creation tasks showed gains) and relative performance of humans vs. AI. The paper found evidence of publication bias favoring studies showing human augmentation (p = 0.002) but not for synergy outcomes. High heterogeneity was observed (I² = 97.7% for synergy, 93.8% for augmentation). The sample sizes reported (n = 34 for creation tasks) refer to the number of studies in subgroups, not individual participant counts. Most human-AI systems involved humans making final decisions after receiving AI input.
